author: jeremyphoward
text: There's a bunch of different versions of the story. But in general, "smart techie with time on his hands spends months creating a useful custom cure for a loved one" is something that we've seen happen many times over the last 20 years. It's not prima facie unusual/unlikely.

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The solution to societal destabilization due to massive increases in power is to give that power to a small number of good people—such as people who are really rich, powerful, and good at getting people to give them power.

(That's how society actually ran for centuries BTW.)

author: jeremyphoward
text:
So ASI alignment is only interesting and important if we can *first* achieve political and social alignment of near-ASI technology.

(If we navigate that successfully, then we'll actually be better placed to handle ASI as a global community anyway.)

author: jeremyphoward

If A is better than placebo, and B is better than A, then B is better than placebo. Therefore we don't need to compare B to placebo, if we have a comparison of B with A.

text:

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I believe that, on the whole, the positive Australian masculine values I grew up with are still largely held by young Aussies today.

But it looks like we're going to have to fight hard to keep them.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Of course, if someone starts fighting you, you should fight back (ideally, harder than they started it!)

Being respectful doesn't mean being a pushover.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I recall having similar interactions online with Claire. When we've disagreed on things (and we seem to disagree on a *lot* of things!) we done so with facts, logic, and respect.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Talking of Balaji reminds me of something else.

Strong men listen and engage with respect. For instance, I disagreed with plenty of stuff Balaji said in the interview, and vice versa. But we talked it through, thoughtfully and respectfully. No name calling. No BS.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
BTW, many of these good masculine traits are also good feminine traits!

E.g another of Balaji's Aussie guests, 
@clairlemon
, continued to point out masks' utility, despite the unpopularity of this position.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Before 
@balajis
 interviewed me, the very first thing he asked was "Jeremy, would you like me to wear a mask? Your family health is very important."

This is manly. Balaji's looking after a friend, and being prepared to also be different in order to do so.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Real Australian men care about looking after the health of others.

Our most respected war hero, Weary Dunlop, was a surgeon, not a fighter, who saved many lives under the most horrifying of conditions.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Adam Goodes, a top footy player, was named Australian of the Year for anti-racism advocacy and promoting indigenous cultural pride.

Aussies love Kurt Fearnley, a top Paralympic wheelchair athlete (also Aussie of the Year), & he's a big media star here.

Being different is fine!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
In the Australia I grew up in (quite some years ago!), making sacrifices to look after your family was widely understood to be something a quintessential Aussie male does.

And being ok with being different has also always been something our strong male role models showed.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The #1 reason I wear a mask is to protect my immunocompromised family member. She nearly died of an infection some years ago, and I'm gonna do everything I can to make sure that does happen again--even if that means looking different and standing out.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Let's take the example from the post, mask-wearing, as an example. Very few people today wear a mask. Wearing a mask, therefore, requires some strength of character--the desire to conform is natural, and hard to counter.

I don't like wearing masks at all. So why do I do it?

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I'm worried that many young people today believe that it's manly to not care. To sit around gaming all day. To care only about money. To be violent.

When I grew up in Australia, none of these were considered masculine traits. I don't want to see these views infect Australia.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
This created a self-reinforcing feedback loop. The more in-distribution tokens ChatGPT was getting in its chat history, the more strongly the auto-regressive model was pushed to stay in that distribution.

ChatGPT memory made this even worse, letting it happen across chats.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Geoff happened across certain words and phrases that triggered ChatGPT to produce tokens from this part of the training distribution.

And the tokens it produced triggered Geoff in turn. That's not a coincidence, the collaboratively-produced fanfic is meant to be compelling!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The SCP wiki is really big -- about 30x bigger than the whole Harry Potter series, at >30 million words!

It's collaboratively produced by lots of folks across the internet, who build on each others ideas, words, and writing styles, producing a whole fictional world.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
For folks wondering what's happening here technically, an explainer:

When there's lots of training data with a particular style, using a similar style in your prompt will trigger the LLM to respond in that style. In this case, there's LOADS of fanfic: 

https://
scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-series

text: We also write some docs pages specifically designed for LLMs -- more concise and code-heavy than when we write for humans. E.g

author: jeremyphoward
text: The idea is that llms.txt is manually created/curated to have the exact info an LLM would find most useful. It's a bit of work to get right, but in my experience it's extremely helpful in practice (all our projects have carefully written llms.txt files).

author: jeremyphoward
text: If you want to full set of info, we recommend using the python tool we provide to create a version of the file with the links inlined, and tell your users how to use it. E.g we do this for fasthtml.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
For small/simple tools, you could just dump all the concise needed info straight into llms.txt, but for larger systems it might not fit even in 200k tokens.

Also, all current LLMs degrade a *lot* beyond 100k tokens in practice.

author: jeremyphoward
text: It was written on the assumption that LLMs would use an agentic-search approach to follow the links that seem most useful. That's why each link has a description of what's there, so the LLM knows where to find the info it needs.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
When you're working well with AI, you have it gently helping you go with your natural flow. It's *your* nature that is the foundation, not the AI's.

"Vibe coding" today isn't like this at all. It's a battle against the AI to control it under your will.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Also there's the later "therefore" part which corresponds more closely to "the way of code": "是以聖人處無為之事，行不言之教".

"無為" is *not* "without labor". It's "without forcing things".

I think *this* can teach us a lot about how to work with AI in fact.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
When 
@seb_ruder
 and I wrote up ULMFiT in a paper, Seb coined the term "universal models" to describe this new kind of model.

Unfortunately later authors didn't follow this and instead coined new terms, despite academic norms. So now we have a mish-mash of poorly defined terms :(

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I got push-back from pretty much everyone about this. My claim that fine-tuning that model was the critical step to achieving success in NLP was not something people were ready to hear at that time.

I gave many talks trying to convince academics to pursue this direction.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
There have been many other important contributions, including attention (Bahdanau et al), transformers, RLHF, etc.

But before all this, basically everyone in NLP assumed that each new domain needed a new model. ULMFiT showed that a large pretrained model was actually the key.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I'm glad 
@levelsio
 checked this, but sad our contrib has been erased by later big tech co's. Alec Radford said ULMFiT inspired GPT. ULMFiT's first demo predated BERT.

Today's 3-stage LLM approach of general corpus pretraining and 2 stages of fine-tuning was pioneered by ULMFiT.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Ready to try fasttransform? Install with `pip install fasttransform`.

Full documentation & examples at: 
https://
answerdotai.github.io/fasttransform/

As 
@HamelHusain
 says, "look at your data" - fasttransform makes this easier than ever!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Multiple dispatch also makes fasttransform highly extensible. Anyone can add behavior to existing transforms without modifying the original code.

For example 
@benjamin_warner
’s fastxtend extends fastai with faster FFCV data loaders for computer vision.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
fasttransform uses multiple dispatch to handle different data types in a single pipeline. Images, labels, masks - each gets the right transform automatically.

No more separate pipelines for inputs & targets that need to stay in sync.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Image segmentation shows another reason why this matters: transforms must be applied identically to both image & mask. With fasttransform, random augmentations stay perfectly aligned - much easier than with separate pipelines.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Other libraries like PyTorch require manually maintaining separate inverse functions to see your transformed data.

With fasttransform, just define your encode/decode functions, & your pipelines will automatically pick them up.

See more in the docs: 
https://
answerdotai.github.io/fasttransform/

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The classic wolf/husky dataset shows a model detecting snow instead of wolves! The model fails when wolves appear without snow or huskies with it.

fasttransform (used in fastai) makes visualizing these issues trivial with just one line of code!

author: jeremyphoward
text: Of course what really matters ends up being the KV activation values, since that’s what’s used in the attention calculation. If your initial tokens end up creating activations that don’t have good autoregressive outputs, they’re low quality activations.

author: jeremyphoward
text: We can’t change Claude’s weights or inference process, so all we can do is provide better tokens. And you only get to provide the initial tokens — after that the ones created autoregressively are out of your control.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Activations are calculated from two inputs: weights, and previous tokens. To get better results, you need either better weights, or better previous tokens, or a better inference algorithm. These are your only options.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Anyway, I'm writing about this here because it's the kind of thing that can be hard to get a straight answer from drs about, since their training is generally pretty focused on cigarette harms, and nicotine patches etc are marketed as smoking cessation tools.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The key differences are how long it takes to feel the impact, and how long it lasts. As a base, patches seem like the best option, since they provide steady nicotine over a 16 or 24 hour period (depending on the patch).

The other options are used to get a 1 hour "focus hit".

author: jeremyphoward
text: In Australia at least, nicotine can be purchased over the counter in lozenge and patch form, and pouches can be purchased online with a prescription (which can also be obtained online). Another option is vaping, although a recent study suggested vaping is as harmful as smoking.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I haven't found any studies of nicotine patches or lozenges which have documented any harms, including any negative effects on the cardio-vascular or respiratory systems.

Nicotine is, however, addictive -- although I haven't seen any reports of non-smoker addiction to patches.

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward

Grok is wrong.

`observation_ring*255` is the 1st op, & that creates & returns a *new* tensor. So the manipulations are occurring on a new tensor, not on something being used by the program being debugged.

I would recommend thinking for yourself, instead of outsourcing to grok.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Yup, you can have a validation set of incomplete documents which you just generate a single next token for each one at temp 0, and get your accuracy from that.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I don't think I've ever seen a real world dataset where this didn't apply.

author: jeremyphoward
text: If the validation set is totally random or totally unrelated, then it won't change. Otherwise it will. Try it! :D

author: jeremyphoward
text: That is -- it will also increase its confidence in the validation set, not just the training set.

author: jeremyphoward
text: No, doing more epochs will always result in the model becoming over-confident, and will therefore result in decreasing validation loss.

author: jeremyphoward
text: It’s not a probability, it’s binary: 1 if the highest logit token is correct.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Validation per token accuracy will continue to improve for many epochs after validation loss appears to be getting worse. That’s because the probability calibration gets worse, not because the predictions are worse.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Remember, loss is a differentiable *proxy* for what you actually care about. 

Token accuracy is much closer to what you should *actually* care about.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Folks seem to rediscover this every couple of years. 

As I’ve been saying for many years, you have to track token accuracy, not loss/perplexity, otherwise you’ll wrongly think the validation loss going up is a bad thing.

text: For loads more details about everything in ModernBERT read the blog post:

author: jeremyphoward
text:
ModernBERT shows encoder models can be improved by modern methods. They offer strong performance, with great size/performance.

We’re really looking forward to seeing what creative ways to use you all come up with! We’re opening a call for demos, with prizes.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text:
A big aspect in which encoders have been trailing is training data, and particularly data *diversity*. Many older models use limited corpora, generally Wikipedia/Wikibooks.

But ModernBERT is trained on a variety of data sources, including web docs, code, & scientific articles.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
ModernBERT takes inspiration from the Transformer++ (from Mamba), including using RoPE & GeGLU, removing unnecessary bias terms, & adding an extra norm layer after embeddings.

Then, we added Alternating Attention (very impactful!), Sequence Packing, & Hardware-Aware Design

author: jeremyphoward
text:
So how does it work? ModernBERT brings modern engineering ideas from LLMs over to encoder models. We did so in three core ways: 1) a modernized transformer architecture; 2) particular attention to efficiency; 3) modern data scales & sources.

Details here: 
https://
arxiv.org/abs/2412.13663

author: jeremyphoward
text:
For code retrieval, ModernBERT is unique. There’s never been an encoder model trained on such a large amount of code data before. E.g on StackOverflow-QA, a hybrid dataset with code and natural language, it's the 1st to score >80.

What new applications will you create with this?

author: jeremyphoward
text:
And ModernBERT is *efficient*.

It’s twice as fast as DeBERTa; up to 4x faster in the more common situation where inputs are mixed length. Long context inference is ~3x faster than other high-quality models.

And it uses less than 1/5th of Deberta’s memory!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The BERT family might not be trendy. But people *actually use them*.

E.g. RoBERTa, one of the leading BERT-based models, has more downloads than the 10 most popular LLMs on HuggingFace combined. In fact, BERT-style models add up to over a billion downloads per month!

author: jeremyphoward
text: GPT & friends are hamstrung: as generative models, they are mathematically “not allowed” to “peek” at later tokens. They can only ever look *backwards*. This is in contrast to encoder-only models like BERT, which are trained so each token can look forwards *and* backwards.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The popular buzz around GenAI has obscured the role of encoder-only models. These are the workhorses of practical language processing, the models that are actually being used for such workloads right now in many scientific and commercial applications.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Fancy GenAI stuff like GPT 4 is too big, slow, private, and expensive for many jobs. Consider that the original GPT-1 was 117m params. Llama 3.1, by contrast, has up to 405 billion params! 

These models are slow, expensive, and *not yours to control*.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text:
We created ModernBERT because encoder-only architectures are ideal for the kinds of real-world problems that come up every day, like retrieval (e.g. for RAG), classification (such as content moderation), & entity extraction.

But they're under-appreciated, with little investment.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text:
ModernBERT is available as a slot-in replacement for any BERT-like model, with both 139M param and 395M param sizes.

It has a 8192 sequence length, is extremely efficient, is uniquely great at analyzing code, and much more. Read this for details:

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I'll get straight to the point.

We trained 2 new models. Like BERT, but modern. ModernBERT.

Not some hypey GenAI thing, but a proper workhorse model, for retrieval, classification, etc. Real practical stuff.

text: My wife is doing a masters in immunology. The students in the course learn a *lot* about viruses. The information is provided in books in forms that the authors endeavor to make the information as understandable as possible, and teachers help transmit that knowledge to students.

text: We do not ban these things (although historically many have tried, in various forms) because enlightened society believes spreading these capabilities widely is safer than limiting them to the rich and powerful.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Books, schools, computers, and spoken language can help experts with the operational planning of reproducing a known biological threat.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
tldr: the issue is that damaging open source is enormously damaging to safety and security.

This has been true of much of computer security, but it's doubly so for AI, and doubly important.

Since AI is potentially dangerous, we shouldn't make it a rivalrous source of power.

Feel free to use AI to help contribute to any of my projects.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The llms.txt file is unusual in that it uses Markdown to structure the information rather than a classic structured format such as XML. The reason for this is that we expect many of these files to be read by language models and agents.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The FastHTML project follows these two proposals for its documentation. For instance, here is the FastHTML docs llms.txt: 
https://
docs.fastht.ml/llms.txt
And an LLM-ready context file: 
https://
docs.fastht.ml/llms-ctx.txt
All nbdev projects now create .md versions of all pages by default.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
We propose that those interested in providing LLM-friendly content add a /llms.txt file to their site.

llms.txt markdown is human and LLM readable, but is also in a precise format allowing fixed processing methods.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Providing information for language models is different to providing information for humans. Language models generally like to have information in a more concise form, and they can ingest a lot of information quickly.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Today websites are not just used to provide information to people, but they are also used to provide information to large language models.

For instance, language models are often used to enhance development environments used by coders.

author: jeremyphoward
text: We're providing a website with details of the proposal, & javascript and python parsers. There's also an example of how to incorporate llms.txt into an editor--rather than weight into the emacs vs vim vs vscode wars, we picked ed, the standard text editor

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Today 
@answerdotai
 is proposing `/llms.txt`. This is a file you can use to tell models where to find LLM-friendly content for your website.

It provides background information, along with links to markdown files providing more detailed information.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
2. Feed the LLM carefully crafted sets of ideas, & have it help you consider and build on their implications. Telling the LLM that "we're just brainstorming" can help move it towards interpolating less common subgraphs.

AlphaGeometry, for instance, used these ideas successfully

author: jeremyphoward
text:
So, how *can* you take advantage of LLMs in your research. There's 2 good ways I know of:

1. Take advantage of the scale of LLMs' interpolation capabilities, by feeding it loads of semi-random and/or evolving prompts, & have an automatic answer-checker, or...

author: jeremyphoward
text: Because LLMs are fuzzy subgraph matchers, they just interpolate what's already there, and they're heavily weighted to what's most common in their training set -- which is *not* the crazy idea that everyone assumes won't work!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
But some humans, some of the time, are able to tenaciously and creatively explore out of the box ideas, even when no-one supports them.

Like Katalin Karikó, for instance.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
To be fair, it's not just LLMs. This is true of the research community too.

For instance, 
@planetmoney
's latest ep includes an interview with a Nobel prizewinning economist who was called a "charlatan" by reviewers when he first published his results.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Absolutely any time I try to explore something even slightly against commonly accepted beliefs, LLMs *always* just rehash the commonly accepted beliefs.

As a researcher, I find this behaviour worse than unhelpful. It gives the mistaken impression that there's nothing to explore

author: jeremyphoward
text:
In other words (to over-simplify a bit), LLMs largely work by fuzzily finding & interpolating concepts found in their training sets.

Because of the fuzziness of the search, and scale of the training set, this can give some pretty impressive results.

But!...

author: jeremyphoward
text:
It's not helpful to just say "LLMs can't reason", since clearly they do some things which humans would use reasoning for.

But LLMs are, first and foremost, fuzzy subgraph matching machines (at *massive* scale*).

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Breakthrough research requires either:

1. Going in a totally new and unexpected direction that everyone decided long ago was stupid, or
2. Finding some extraordinary new experimental data that means we have to change our theories

LLMs can't run experiments, so we'll focus on 1

author: jeremyphoward
text:
For those that hope (or worry) that LLMs will do breakthrough scientific research, I've got good (or bad) news:

LLMs are particularly, exceedingly, marvellously ill-suited to this task. (if you're a researcher, you'll have noticed this already)

Here's why

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Your reaction to this fact might be "OK then, let's just agree that AI shouldn't be open sourced; we should restrict model access to only employees of big tech".

If that's what's on the table, then let's discuss it.

But let's not pretend we don't have to choose.

author: jeremyphoward
text: There are two ways in which it's possible to regulate AI whilst also allowing open source.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
The fact that these two things can't both be true is not a political or advocacy statement -- it's just a simple statement of reality.

Because this inconsistency is not understood by policy makers, the current discussion of AI policy is about something that can't exist.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
In talking to policy makers and AI researchers, I realised there's a fact agreed upon by all researchers, but understood by almost no policy makers.

This uncomfortable fact is why AI policy is hard.

text: PPS: If you want to keep doing things the dumb way, that's totes fine. You do you! But don't feel like you need to tell me about it.

author: jeremyphoward
text: PS: Yes I know what everyone says about `import *`. They're just repeating what they've been told. It's fine, really. Especially if you define `__all__`, which you should, and which is done automatically if you use nbdev (which you also should).

author: jeremyphoward
text:
`replace_wildcards` scans your file for any symbols that are defined in a wildcard imported module, and used in your file. It replaces `import *` with explicit symbol import.

To continue being productive, you can add `import *` back after pushing to main, and re-run it later.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
If you prefer getting shit done over pointless busywork, then you might like 'import *'.

But your boss might like busywork, and demand you don't use 'import *'.

Well now you can *both* be happy! Just install fastcore, and type `replace_wildcards <file>`

fastcore.fast.ai

author: jeremyphoward
text: If you want to create an "agent" that combines multiple tools, or run the same tool model times in a sequence, in order to solve a more complex problem, it's a single line of code in Claudette

author: jeremyphoward
text: For function calling, Claudette is unique in allowing you to use nothing by Python types and comments to fully specify your schema. No JSON, no Pydantic, just plain python functions!

author: jeremyphoward
text: But you can also do stuff you can't do in the web app at all, like "prefill" -- forcing Claude to start its response with whatever you want.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Instead of "p(doom)", let's consider "p(salvation)" too, and bring a new concept to the AI safety discussion:

“Human Existential Enhancement Factor” (HEEF): the degree to which AI enhances our ability to overcome existential threats and ensure our long-term well-being.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I don't propose that we exempt AI from regulation. However, we should be careful to regulate with an understanding of the delicate balance between control and centralization, vs transparency and access, as we've done with other technologies throughout history.

author: jeremyphoward
text: If we can't fine-tune open sourced models, then we'll be stuck with whatever values and aims the model creators had. Chinese propaganda is a very real current example of this issue (and remember that the best current open source models are Chinese).

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Regulating the deployment of services, instead of the release of models, would not impact big tech at all, since they rarely (if ever) release large models.

So the big tech companies would be just as covered as before, and open source would be protected.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Thankfully, the issues in SB 1047 can all easily be fixed by legislating the deployment of “AI Systems” and not legislating the release of “AI Models”.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
SB 1047 does not cover "base models". But these are the models where >99% of compute is used. By not covering these models, the bill will probably actually not cover any models at all.

(There are also dozens of trivial workarounds for anyone wanting to train uncovered models.)

author: jeremyphoward
text:
This is important, so don't just read this thread, instead read the 6000+ word article I just published.

In the article I explain how AI *actually* works, and why these details totally break legislation like SB 1047. Policy makers *need* to know this:

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I've done a deep dive into SB 1047 over the last few weeks, and here's what you need to know:

*Nobody* should be supporting this bill in its current state. It will *not* actually cover the largest models, nor will it actually protect open source.

But it can be easily fixed!

author: jeremyphoward
text: FWIW, personally I don't identify as "left" or "right" and know very very few people who do. I don't think they're particularly useful labels for people, on the whole -- they can be somewhat useful labels for ideas however.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Yes I know. I'm describing a particular result of a particular theory. There are other theories that have the same result, as you say.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I don't think you're being insulting btw -- because I don't think almost anyone would associate to the kind of extreme position that you're reacting to.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
tbh this essay has always felt like "save the children" -- it's such an obvious and easy thing to say and gets heads nodding, but there's not really much under the surface.

Everyone wants to believe they're "moderate". And everyone else is "extreme".

author: jeremyphoward
text: Also, I've generally found that "leftist" oriented people tend to be strong supporters of open source, because there's a modern leftist theory that economies of scale and positive feedback loops can lead to over-centralization of power. I think this makes a lot of sense.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
For instance, in Australia at least, the left is associated with a view that justice should be available to people regardless of ethnicity, and as a result there's a lot of protests against the genocide in Gaza.

I think they're based on a strong connection to reality.

author: jeremyphoward
text: OK. I guess I don't know what specific set of theories you mean by "modern leftism". There are certainly some theories that are both modern and would generally be considered leftist which seem especially bad, but not all of them!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Apologies that wasn't my intention. So you're introducing a 3rd category of people who are not towards either end of that spectrum?

It also sounds like you're using more of a political compass approach to defining left vs right? But then how are you aligning that?

author: jeremyphoward
text: Indeed. In general, I don't know that there's really such a strong correlation between accuracy of theories and political spectrum. In different places, and different times, theories on both sides of the spectrum have been right at times and wrong at times.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Generally "leftist" means progressive, and "rightest" (i.e the alternative) means conservative.

Are you using them to mean something else?

author: jeremyphoward
text: I don't think your contention is logically consistent, or matches observed data.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I content that there exist people in the world who might self-describe (or you might describe) as "leftist", who do not meet your other criteria.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Research strongly suggests that actually conservatism tends to be associated with an unreasonably strong belief that the world is fair. It's not really a position that's always backed up by reality.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I mean yes, obviously there's a metric fuck-ton of people who *do* meet your criteria. But *all* people who have a progressive view (which is generally what "leftist" means)? There's some reason why being attached to the status quo that means one has a monopoly on truth?

author: jeremyphoward
text: Dude I'm surprised that you go for such simplistic labels. Do you *really* think there's a whole swathe of humanity who can be so simply described in those terms? Often this is used as a cop-out to avoid the actual complexity of the world and the people in it.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text: Fair point - I guess what's new is that it has a name (at least I didn't see a name for this research niche before), and that a lot of people are working on it now.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text:
Amongst the people I know who use Jupyter for most of their work, the majority have chosen to use nbclassic. Also, lots of folks are now using vscode's notebook too, especially for the great copilot integration.

So different folks are making different choices for their needs.

author: jeremyphoward
text: And because extensions are easy to build, and so many people have used nbclassic for so long, that for anything in the base software that could be tweaked or tuned, there's an extension for that!

author: jeremyphoward
text: Also, the many many years of real-world use of nbclassic really shows -- it's really fast, nearly bug-free in practice, and is full of delightful little touches to make my experience pleasant and productive.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I have loads of shortcuts and processes for working effectively with my browser, so I really appreciate software that leverages my browser's features, as nbclassic does.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I really like the simplicity of the extensions system in nbclassic, and I can even develop and test extensions from within a notebook itself.

I also love the interface, which relies on using the browser's native tools (e.g. tabs) instead of having a separate implementation.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I've spent about 500 hours working with JupyterLab, and it's an impressive piece of software, and many people will find it best meets their needs.

author: jeremyphoward

BTW the reason I added that diagram feature is that there's so little software around that does this *properly* -- that is, actually showing *which* field the foreign and primary keys point to.

And those that do are often expensive, or require a big java install, or both!

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Naturally, everyone wants to jump on this bandwagon. And, in a sense, it's a great coup to all the people who worked so hard and so effectively to get to this point.

But, oddly enough, the extraordinary success of this project has resulted in a more fragile outcome.

author: jeremyphoward
text: This work has been presented beautifully, and of course the company itself has been massively financially successful. Folks that work there have very high social standing in the bay area.

author: jeremyphoward
text: This has gone so well that there's now a major group-think problem. Anthropic in particular (based partly on EA/LW/etc history) have introduced their own vocabulary, their own context around what problems are worth solving, and their own ways of solving those problems.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The reason for this, counter-intuitively, is due to the exceptional work done by EA, OP, and Anthropic folks over the last few years. Groups like 80000 hours and the AIS course are pushing enormous numbers of young people in very specific directions.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Thanks for the question Neel. I don't actually want to promote any particular research right now, but instead would prefer to focus on the more important issue: the lack of resilience, leading to fragility, and therefore reduced safety, due to too much concentration of effort.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Low-hanging fruit means you can get some cheap cites if you're fast to publish. But that doesn't count as great research IMHO -- it's just doing something that would have been done anyway, but a little faster.

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Prefill messages (i.e. a final assistant-role message containing the desired start of the response) are nearly always entirely ignored by GPT-4o.

All previous models have treated them as the response start, like Claude and Gemini Pro both do.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Unfortunately, prefill is completely broken in GPT-4o.

I'm not blaming OpenAI for this really, because it was never documented -- it just happened to work.

But so much of my work totally depends on prefill, so this is a big problem. 
@OpenAI
 any chance you could fix it?

author: jeremyphoward
text:
If you're wondering: {JSON,X}Path are mini languages for searching tree-structured data in XML, JSON, or HTML format to find nodes or subtrees of interest.

For instance, `//input/
@value
` would get the values of all <input> elements in a form, at any DOM tree depth.

author: jeremyphoward
text: This needs to be authentic. If the LanceDB creator for example doesn't look at your work and feel excited about what you've done, and you haven't clearly explained what's so great about it, they won't want to re-share it.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Finally -- when you highlight someone's work, tell them about it! If they like it, they will become your viral marketing team... :) E.g tag the creator of LanceDB in a tweet, saying why you've highlighted that library, linking to the part of your post explaining how to use it.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
This is a big reason why I try to teach "top down" -- start by using the cool thing, and then gradually dig into how it works.

Otherwise you don't get the sweet cool thing until the end -- your audience needs a *lot* of trust in you to go that whole journey then!

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Another key missing thing for me was a clear pointer as to what the outcome would be up-front. Will you show how to create something new? Will that new thing be clearly better than the old version of that thing?

Provide a concrete and easy to understand sample of the output.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Having said that, the actual approach was strong. For instance, I only learned about LanceDB recently, and I think it's great that you're teaching people about it.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I felt overall like there was too much prose, and too little code. In the end, I didn't read the whole thing, but tried to pick out the bits that were likely to teach me something. Finding those bits, and pulling out the key ideas, was challenging.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Last year I warned about the dangers of regulations of these kinds. I'm really disappointed that these warnings are not being heeded.

author: jeremyphoward
text: SB-1047 creates significant barriers to entry for small businesses and startups looking to innovate in AI. Compliance costs & legal risks could deter entrepreneurs. This would stifle innovation and concentrate power within established corporations

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Harming open source will harm developers, consumers, academics, and obstruct the development of new startups, due to:
- Overly broad definitions
- Misunderstanding of dual use
- Restrictive requirements
- Disincentivizing Openness

author: jeremyphoward
text:
By imposing restrictions on open-source AI, SB1047 hurts AI safety, reducing:
- Collaboration, which allows a wider range of experts to identify and address potential safety concerns
- Resilience; concentrating control creates single points of failure & increases systemic risk

author: jeremyphoward
text:

FSDP/QDoRA is just as memory efficient and scalable as FSDP/QLoRA, and critically is *also* as accurate for continued pre-training as full weight training.

If you haven't read the DoRA paper yet, now's a good time! (It's a clear and well written paper.)

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I don't know where you're seeing claims that any model is harmless. It's like describing a pencil as harmless, or education as harmless - that's not really the key claim.

The key claim is that equal access to such things is societally important, and unequal access is unstable.

author: jeremyphoward
text: This is complex and difficult. Consider in detail the first, second, and third degree impacts of what you advocate. Then remember, first do no harm.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Please stop saying ostensibly harmless. It's a strawman and strawmen are unhelpful. 

I understand your desire for humanity to take a breath, but all interventions I'm aware of that are designed to make that happen seem more likely to increase fragility and instability.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
It requires no such assumption. It is a question of power. Do you want the greatest power in the world to be in competition amongst profit seeking companies, or do you want it democratised?

Which is safer?

It does seem to have been an issue throughout history -- in reading biographies and histories of tech innovation, it does seem often the folks that first build things can fade into the background, vs the folks that come later.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The fact that Nick Gruen was the founding chairman, and I was the founding president, isn't likely to be mentioned by the team of the company at the time it's being acquired. So all the new column inches will be about the team at that time, based on what they want to present.

author: jeremyphoward
text: In this case, the underlying issue appears to be that Kaggle got lots of attention when it was acquired, and the execs at that time did PR stuff. So writing about the company after the acquisition is focused on the POV of those execs, rather than historical accuracy.

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward
text:
When we trained ULMFiT in 2017 we used validation set token accuracy to track performance.

This is *much* more useful that tracking loss. Eval loss goes up because the model becomes over-confident -- but it's not becoming less accurate. So simply track token accuracy!

author: jeremyphoward

author: jeremyphoward
text: Having said that, I think the more likely source of "leakage" is that GPT4 will have likely seen these benchmark datasets, or something much like them, and in generating synthetic data would be likely to return stuff that's very similar.

text: Because something can be trained to reason doesn't mean it's not reasoning!

author: jeremyphoward
text: Anything looking only at untuned LLMs can never really say anything interesting about capabilities, because you're looking at an accidental artifact of the SSL process -- not something that's designed to be used directly. What it can or can't do is of no impact.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I certainly don't see how your work supports this claim -- I think my view of the task is more compatible with both what you are seeing, and the literature results more generally.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I think that's a bold claim and needs serious support! I can't see how the complex tasks in many datasets could be solved without reasoning -- or at least how they could be solved through some mindless access of ICL.

author: jeremyphoward
text: The *only* reason we create those LLMs is because we can do self-supervised learning using token cross-entropy -- we don't need to collect expensive labels. And this SSL tasks just happens to create rich latent capabilities.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I read that discussion, and I understand your claim.

My claim is the opposite - that this isn't really testing abilities at all. It's testing things that just so happen to be observable in an untuned base LLM.

But untuned base LLMs were never meant to be used directly.

author: jeremyphoward
text: I think it also depends on how much fine-tuning it takes. If it takes a single GPU for a few hours, then clearly that capability was already there - it just needed to be surfaced.

author: jeremyphoward
text: My view is that that's not a particularly useful or interesting thing to check.

author: jeremyphoward
text: Using your definition, you're not testing whether those latent capabilities are already there ready to be quickly surfaced. Instead you're testing whether they so happen to be already expressed by LM pretraining.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Ulmfit showed that guess was correct. And instruction tuning shows even quite powerful and general capabilities can be surfaced very quickly. 

That's because they were already there, and just needed a suitable projection.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
I don't think this premise tests the correct thing.

Plain LMs don't necessarily have any abilities beyond the ability to predict the next word - but the reason we created fine tuned LMs is because we believed they would have latent abilities that would be easy to surface.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
Ah well that's a very long story. The key insights I only recently realised are that:
- Creating a single superhuman AI isn't that impactful; it has to outperform all human society, and
- You can't actually just copy AIs, because each requires energy.

author: jeremyphoward
text: For me perhaps the more important question is: how long does this memorization last for? For a really large input, eventually it'll start forgetting. So perhaps in that case you want to train on the most important items to memorize last? (And visa versa!)

author: jeremyphoward
text: Regarding "reshuffle using the LR schedule as a PDF" - that's a clever idea, but I doubt it'll make any significant difference. There's no real sign it matters too much whether every input is seen with the exact same frequency.

author: jeremyphoward
text: To answer "does reshuffling across epochs help" - I'm not sure any "help" is needed. We're not actually seeing a problem. The accuracy is improving as we'd hope - at least until we use up all the juice in the dataset. There's no sign of accuracy getting significantly worse.

author: jeremyphoward
text:
This "rapid memorization" issue seems like something that's part of the collective knowledge of the LLM fine-tuning community, but we haven't seen it explicitly studied or written up before.

(We may have missed prior work however, so please let us know if we did!)

author: jeremyphoward
text: If you come up with any alternative hypotheses, & are able to test them, or you find any empirical evidence that the memorization hypothesis is wrong, let us know; & tell us if you have ideas about how we should adjust how we train and use these models based on these observations

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text:
There is no fundamental law that says that neural networks can’t learn to recognise inputs from a single example. It’s just what researchers and practitioners have generally found to be the case in practice.

We know that some things can make loss surfaces smoother, like resnets.

author: jeremyphoward
jeremyphoward
text: It’s important to note that the validation loss getting worse doesn’t mean that we’re over-fitting in practice. What we generally care about is accuracy, and it’s fine if the model is over-confident. Our accuracy keeps improving during training even as validation loss gets worse

author: jeremyphoward
text:
To test it, we used the classic cyclical learning rate (CLR) schedule for two epochs.

The resulting loss curve, as far as we can tell, can only be explained by nearly complete memorisation occurring from a single example!

https://
arxiv.org/abs/1506.01186

author: jeremyphoward
text: The hypothesis that we kept hearing from open source colleagues is that that these training curves were actually showing overfitting. This seemed, at first, quite impossible. It would imply that the model was learning to recognise inputs from just 1 or 2 examples!

author: jeremyphoward
text: But we checked the code carefully, and also reached out to the open source fine-tuning community, and were provided with lots of examples of similar training curves with a wide variety of training loops and datasets.
